title: New Labour, new leaders? Gendering transformational leadership
abstract: Abstract Transformational leadership is widely recognised as being central to the implementation of educational reform. In this paper I draw on selected educational speeches made by New Labour politicians in order to locate shifting discourses of leadership within the broader accountability framework through which the terms of the relationship between central government and head teachers have been re/configured in the United Kingdom. The gendered politics of transformation are examined, highlighting new and renewed forms of masculinity embedded within new leadership ideals. It is suggested that a gendered critique of transformational leadership offers an important contribution to critical analyses of the neo‐liberal and managerialist educational project. Notes 1. The time frame was selected in relation to a wider study of which the analysis of New Labour speeches formed a part. An ethnographic investigation of one secondary school was carried out in parallel to discourse analysis of the speeches in order to more fully explore the effects of educational discourse experienced by the head teacher, teachers and students at a micro‐political level (Lambert, Citation2005). Although speeches made by all these politicians were incorporated in the corpus selected for analysis, a smaller number are explicitly referred to in this article.
journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
